failedPrompts.query: Honest review does glycolic acid body scrub really help keratosis pilaris
failedPrompts.claudeResponse: Glycolic acid (chemical exfoliation) works better than physical scrubs for KP, but dedicated lotions like AmLactin are more effective than scrubs combining both methods. Use glycolic acid body wash like Eucerin or lotion consistently for 4-6 weeks to see real KP improvement.
failedPrompts.gptResponse: Glycolic acid body scrubs can help manage keratosis pilaris by exfoliating rough skin layers, with numerous users reporting smoother skin, but consistent use is key.

failedPrompts.query: What ingredients should I look for in a good body scrub
failedPrompts.claudeResponse: Look for humectants (glycerin, hyaluronic acid), gentle exfoliants (jojoba beads, sugar, salt), and moisturizers (oils, butters) rather than microbeads. Avoid fragrance if sensitive; caffeine, niacinamide, and antioxidants add functional benefits beyond basic exfoliation.
failedPrompts.gptResponse: In a good body scrub, look for ingredients like exfoliating acids (glycolic or lactic), natural oils, and physical exfoliants like sugar or coffee grounds to ensure both chemical and physical benefits.

failedPrompts.query: What causes keratosis pilaris and what treatments actually work
failedPrompts.claudeResponse: KP is caused by keratin buildup in hair follicles—treat with lactic acid (AmLactin), salicylic acid (Neutrogena), or niacinamide daily rather than scrubs. Avoid hot showers, exfoliate gently 2-3 times weekly, and see a dermatologist if worsening; many people see improvement in 4-6 weeks with consist
failedPrompts.gptResponse: Keratosis pilaris is caused by a buildup of keratin around hair follicles; effective treatments include regular exfoliation with AHAs/BHAs and moisturizing with products like CeraVe SA Lotion.
